texts.orig: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is a separation process in which a hot feed stream is passed over a microporous hydrophobic membrane. The temperature difference between the two sides of the membrane leads to a vapor pressure difference that causes water to evaporate from the hot side and, pass through the pores to the cold side. The vapor is pure water which can be condensed. This process has application to desalting water. Compared to reverse osmosis, MD does not require a high pressure feed, can tolerate complete dryout of the membrane, and can process very high salinity brines. Compared to other large thermal processes, it is easily scalable. Demonstrated pilot plants have been used at a small scale (0.1 m 3 /day), including stand-alone systems disconnected from municipal power or water networks [1 -3].

texts.orig: MD systems can be used in many con fi gurations, depending on how liquid is collected from the permeate side. In direct contact MD (DCMD), the vapor is condensed on a pure water stream that contacts the other side of the membrane. In air gap MD (AGMD), an air gap separates the membrane from a cold condensing plate which collects vapor that moves across the gap. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) has shown potential as a means of desalination and water puri fi cation. As a thermally driven membrane technology which runs at relatively low pressure, which can withstand high salinity feed streams, and which is potentially more resistant to fouling, MD could be used for desalination where reverse osmosis is not a good option. The use of thermal energy, rather than electrical energy, and the fact that MD membranes can withstand dryout make this technology attractive for renewable power applications as well. However, most research on MD has focused on maximizing membrane fl ux as opposed to minimizing energy consumption and cost, and current MD systems suffer from poor energy ef fi ciency compared to other desalination systems. In solar driven systems, the reported thermal performance has not been much better than a simple solar still. This paper examines the energy ef fi ciency of single-stage MDbased desalination cycles in each of the MD con fi gurations commonly used for desalination (direct contact, air gap, and vacuum) and compares the gained output ratio, or GOR, of each con fi guration across the range of membrane module geometries, and operating conditions. Limitations of each con fi guration are identi fi ed. Direct contact MD and air gap MD, in particular, have potential for high GOR.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most research on MD desalination focuses on maximizing membrane fl ux. However some studies have examined energy ef fi ciency for experimental plants at the 0.1 m 3 /day scale [1,2]. Additionally, more recent MD desalination studies have also examined energy ef fi ciency [1,6 -8]. Using membrane fl ux as a proxy for thermal performance may not lead to the correct conclusion about overall system performance, as membrane fl ux is highly dependent on system con fi guration, membrane area, energy input, and heat recovery from hot brine and condensing vapor. In a complete cycle, the highest fl ux may not lead the best use of energy, as it often requires high heat inputs and the resulting high membrane fl ux can increase resistance to heat and mass transfer.

texts.orig: AGMD, and VMD. Alkalibi and Lior compared fl ux over different MD con fi gurations (including SGMD) [10] and expanded their analysis to include heat and mass transfer characteristics [11]. Ding et al. [12] examined the removal of ammonia from water using MD with a focus on fl ux and chemical quantities. However, no studies have examined MD in the context of a full desalination cycle, where energy recovery is highly important to the viability of a desalination process. This paper uses models of MD transport processes coupled with basic cycle single-stage cycles to make a direct comparison of the performance limits of each MD con fi guration. Knowing which con fi guration of MD is most energy ef fi cient will aid designers in fi nding the most cost effective con fi guration for development, experimentation, and optimization in desalination.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. Characteristics of MD configurations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: DCMD systems in desalination have been studied fairly extensively. While they have good salt rejection, they suffer high trans-membrane heat loss, and consequently low membrane fl ux. Typical DCMD systems, tested with NaCl solutions between 35,000 and 100,000 ppm, have achieved a membrane fl ux from 1 to 10 L/ m 2 h [13 -15]. The biggest disadvantage of these systems is that the cool water on the permeate side results in large conductive heat losses through the thin membrane.
texts.text: DCMD systems in desalination have been studied fairly extensively. While they have good salt rejection, they suffer high trans-membrane heat loss, and consequently low membrane fl ux. Typical DCMD systems, tested with NaCl solutions between 35,000 and 100,000 ppm, have achieved a membrane fl ux from 1 to 10 L/ m 2 h [13 -15]. The biggest disadvantage of these systems is that the cool water on the permeate side results in large conductive heat losses through the thin membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AGMD typically has a slightly higher fl ux for similar temperature conditions, as the air gap provides thermal insulation between the hot feed and cold condenser water. However, resistance to mass fl ux is limited by diffusion across the air gap and evaporation through the pores. Condensate fi lm thickness is typically 10 times thinner than the air gap width [10]. Fluxes for AGMD systems operating near 70 °C inlet feed temperature range from 10 L/m 2 h [10,2] up to 65 L/m 2 h [9]. This type of system is most commonly used in pilotscale desalination systems [16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: VMDhasthebestperformancein terms of fl ux as a result of enhancement by the mechanical pressure difference. One system operating at a high temperature of 85 °C [17] achieved a fl ux of 71 L/m 2 h. A second system [9] was able to achieve a consistent fl ux of 146 L/m 2 h operating at 300 Pa on the permeate side and with a 40 °C feed inlet temperature. A third system [18] made use of turbulent feed fl ow and vacuum to enhance fl ux, achieving 40 L/m 2 h. The applied vacuum on the permeate side was roughly half an atmosphere, which can easily be achieved without expensive pumps and pressure vessels. This indicates that a mechanically applied pressure difference is more advantageous when using VMD systems. In cases where fouling is a concern, mechanical pressure enhancement can keep feed temperatures low while still achieving high fl ux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SGMD relies on a sweeping gas to entrain water vapor. It is typically not used in desalination as the latent heat is not given up into the air stream, and adding moisture to an air stream results in an increase in enthalpy without necessarily increasing the bulk temperature. This makes it hard to recover energy into the brine stream, as the air exits close to, or below, the seawater inlet temperature, requiring additional cooling to create the appropriate temperature gradient to transfer heat. The requirement for external cooling has made this cycle too complex and expensive to be realized for desalination, where energy recovery is crucial.

texts.orig: GOR is the ratio of the latent heat of evaporation of a unit mass of product water to the amount of energy used by a desalination system to produce that unit mass of product. The higher the GOR, the better the performance. For example, a solar still would have a GOR on the order of 1 [19], whereas a good Multi-Effect Distillation system may have a GOR of 12 [20]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most experimental systems have achieved a GOR of around 1. Larger membrane areas have achieved higher GOR values. With the limited amount of data on VMD systems reported GORs remain rather low, below 1, when compared to tested DCMD and AGMD designs. A wide range of GOR for each type of system shows the dependence on con fi guration and operating conditions. The systems are all at prototype scale, with an output of 0.1 -1 m 3 /day. These results show the need for additional insight on how the design of each type of MD con fi guration affects the thermal performance, which would in turn affect water cost.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Analytical models of MD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Detailed modeling of the transport processes in an MD module is the fi rst step toward complete cycle modeling, and allows the identifi cation of performance limiting processes at different operating conditions.
texts.text: Detailed modeling of the transport processes in an MD module is the fi rst step toward complete cycle modeling, and allows the identifi cation of performance limiting processes at different operating conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Existing analytical models for MD systems were adapted to be solved numerically, with the membrane divided along its length, L , in the fl ow direction, into cells of length dz and width w , which when multiplied make a differential area element, dA . Bulk fl ows serve as inputs and outputs for neighboring cells. An example cell of differential length for the feed side of any MD con fi guration with heat and mass fl ows illustrated is shown in Fig. 1. In all cases a counter- fl ow con fi guration is used. The equations describing the model were solved as a system of equations using Engineering Equation Solver (EES) [21]. EES is a simultaneous equation solver which uses a Newton iteration method to converge on the solution. For AGMD, models by Liu et al. [22] and Rattner et al. [23] were used as a basis to solve to heat and mass transfer interactions across the cell. For DCMD a model by Bui et al. [24] was used. In the case of VMD, models that re fl ect steady-state operation were used, in which all air and other non-condensable gasses have been removed from the permeate side of the membrane module and the mole fraction of water vapor is close to 1. These formulations are similar to those of Mericq et al. [25] However instead of directly calculating membrane permeability, a constant membrane distillation coef fi cient is used representing the average values from several tested commercial membranes [26 -28]. This value is given in Table 2. Properties for pure water [29] were used and evaluated at the temperature and pressure of the bulk fl ows in each cell. The models were validated with recent experimental data.
texts.text: Existing analytical models for MD systems were adapted to be solved numerically, with the membrane divided along its length, L , in the fl ow direction, into cells of length dz and width w , which when multiplied make a differential area element, dA . Bulk fl ows serve as inputs and outputs for neighboring cells. An example cell of differential length for the feed side of any MD con fi guration with heat and mass fl ows illustrated is shown in Fig. 1. In all cases a counter- fl ow con fi guration is used. The equations describing the model were solved as a system of equations using Engineering Equation Solver (EES) [21]. EES is a simultaneous equation solver which uses a Newton iteration method to converge on the solution. For AGMD, models by Liu et al. [22] and Rattner et al. [23] were used as a basis to solve to heat and mass transfer interactions across the cell. For DCMD a model by Bui et al. [24] was used. In the case of VMD, models that re fl ect steady-state operation were used, in which all air and other non-condensable gasses have been removed from the permeate side of the membrane module and the mole fraction of water vapor is close to 1. These formulations are similar to those of Mericq et al. [25] However instead of directly calculating membrane permeability, a constant membrane distillation coef fi cient is used representing the average values from several tested commercial membranes [26 -28]. This value is given in Table 2. Properties for pure water [29] were used and evaluated at the temperature and pressure of the bulk fl ows in each cell. The models were validated with recent experimental data.

texts.orig: Given the connection between local conditions, such as mean temperature, and local pore size and tortuosity distribution, which is usually unknown, we opt to use a lumped value for B to represent the membrane permeability. Additionally, given the variability of membranes and the focus on cycle performance and energy recovery, a constant value of B is used as determined from values previously obtained by experiment. This reduces the dependence of the cycle performance on the qualities of a speci fi c membrane.

Therefore this parameter would have the greatest effect on the fl ux of a VMD module. As shown in Table 8, the parameter B can vary widely depending on the process used, with VMD resulting in lower B values. Additionally, in smaller modules damage to the membrane has a more pronounced effect on performance. The model cannot take into account local membrane variations, which must be observed experimentally. Variation in B contributes to dif fi culty in validating this model with published results.

texts.orig: 4. Energy consumption results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For comparisons of energy ef fi ciency, the models were coupled with basic single stage cycle designs. The basis for the geometry of the module used for every con fi guration was the one designed by the Fraunhofer Institute [34] and tested in several real-world settings [2,3]. This module is found in the majority of existing solar-powered membrane distillation systems [16]. The dimensions were used in a simple fl at plate module with the baseline attributes listed in Table 2.

texts.orig: In the system models, feed water is assumed to be extracted directly from the sea at 27 °C. Operation is steady and no storage or recirculation of the feed is used. Heat exchangers have no fi xed size or heat transfer coef fi cient, but instead are taken to operate at a fi xed terminal temperature difference (TTD). TTD is the minimum temperature difference between two streams in a heat exchanger, and a value of 3 °C was assumed, which is common in practice. This ensures that the GOR is not limited by the fi xed size of a heat exchanger and the comparison is only between the MD con fi gurations. Figs. 9 -11 show the fl owsheets for these cycles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 15 shows the effect of varying the mass fl ow rate. It shows that GOR can be maximized when the fl ow rate is low. For membranes 10 m long, mass fl ows under 0.2 kg/s yield relatively high GOR. After a certain mass fl ow rate GOR goes below 1 and decreases slowly. This indicates that, for short membrane lengths, fl ow should be maintained in the laminar regime despite the increased heat transfer resistance. In AGMD, however, GOR is very high for a very low mass fl ow rate, as there is very little heat required to raise the feed to its top temperature. Unfortunately, the water production is extremely small, making this con fi guration impractical in the real world. Better results are obtained by increasing the effective length for a higher mass fl ow rate.

texts.orig: increase fl ux in VMD, the amount of cooling water needed to condense the permeate vapor without exceeding the saturation temperature minus the condenser terminal temperature difference becomes very large. Some of this incoming seawater can be rejected as excess cooling water, which decreases the load on the heater, but discards useful energy which otherwise could be returned to the feed. Thus, using excess cooling water results in no net effect on GOR. It can alternatively be passed through the heater, increasing the fl ux and water production; but in that con fi guration the heating requirement outweighs the energy recovery resulting in a net decrease in GOR.

texts.orig: 4.3. Comparison of VMD: use of brine energy recovery
texts.text: 4.3. Comparison of VMD: use of brine energy recovery

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the brine reject in a single-stage system exits at an elevated temperature, it would make sense to recover some of this energy and put it back in the feed. Ideally, all the energy would be recycled by brine recirculation with the injection of makeup water to compensate for the fresh water generated. This is approximated in steady state by an ideal regenerator (no terminal temperature difference) as shown in Fig. 11B. Comparison between a normal single stage VMD system and one with brine regeneration is shown in Figs. 16 -18.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These results demonstrate that, even with ideal brine energy recovery, the GOR of a reduced pressure VMD system reaches a limit of 0.93, which is far below what is expected to be needed for these systems to be economically viable [38]. This is because the low saturation temperature prohibits high temperature rise in the seawater inlet from energy recovery of the permeate vapor, which is essential for increasing GOR.

texts.orig: stage at descending permeate pressure. The cold seawater used to condense the vapor is passed in the opposite direction, gaining temperature as it heads to the fi rst stage with the highest permeate pressure. This arrangement allows similar fl ux as a large single stage VMD, but the higher permeate pressures and corresponding higher saturation temperatures allow heat transfer into the cold seawater at higher temperature reducing the amount of heating required. Recently, a system set up as a multi-effect distillation system which uses membranes to produce vapor instead of direct boiling, achieved a GOR of around 3 in 5 stages [8]. However similar performance might be achieved in single stage AGMD and DCMD system, which may be less expensive to build, and do not require additional energy for vacuum pumps.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper has presented theoretical models for the commonly used con fi gurations of membrane distillation used in desalination: direct contact, air-gap and vacuum. These models have been validated with available experimental data. While the theoretical results show order of magnitude agreement or better, the lack of experimental details, particularly membrane properties, make it dif fi cult to precisely validate the theoretical model against the available experimental results.
texts.text: This paper has presented theoretical models for the commonly used con fi gurations of membrane distillation used in desalination: direct contact, air-gap and vacuum. These models have been validated with available experimental data. While the theoretical results show order of magnitude agreement or better, the lack of experimental details, particularly membrane properties, make it dif fi cult to precisely validate the theoretical model against the available experimental results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More importantly, the models have been used to evaluate the energy ef fi ciency of each common MD configuration in a simple single-stage cycle where energy is recaptured to improve ef fi ciency. From this several important conclusions can be reached:
texts.text: More importantly, the models have been used to evaluate the energy ef fi ciency of each common MD configuration in a simple single-stage cycle where energy is recaptured to improve ef fi ciency. From this several important conclusions can be reached:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Rate limiting processes, and their impact on GOR, can be determined from the model equations.
texts.text: Rate limiting processes, and their impact on GOR, can be determined from the model equations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Single stage VMD, utilizing a permeate stream of pure vapor, is inherently limited by the low temperature of condensation which results from reducing the pressure. This makes it hard to heat the brine to a high temperature in the condenser, and recover heat ef fi ciently.
texts.text: Single stage VMD, utilizing a permeate stream of pure vapor, is inherently limited by the low temperature of condensation which results from reducing the pressure. This makes it hard to heat the brine to a high temperature in the condenser, and recover heat ef fi ciently.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · As a result, the GOR of single stage VMD systems is limited to less than 1, even with ideal heat recovery/recirculation of the brine reject.
texts.text: As a result, the GOR of single stage VMD systems is limited to less than 1, even with ideal heat recovery/recirculation of the brine reject.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · AGMD and DCMD have the potential for high GOR if properly optimized. Local condensation of vapor allows for heat transfer at high temperatures improving heat recovery.
texts.text: AGMD and DCMD have the potential for high GOR if properly optimized. Local condensation of vapor allows for heat transfer at high temperatures improving heat recovery.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · In these systems, GOR is raised by long effective lengths, and low fl ux, which keep resistances to heat and mass transfer small
texts.text: In these systems, GOR is raised by long effective lengths, and low fl ux, which keep resistances to heat and mass transfer small

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · In AGMD, the gap width has the greatest impact on GOR.
texts.text: In AGMD, the gap width has the greatest impact on GOR.
